elements.sent-2411-001: We provide a comprehensive comparison of linear amplifiers and microwave photon-counters in axion dark matter experiments.
elements.sent-2411-002: The study is done assuming a range of realistic operating conditions and detector parameters, over the frequency range between 1--30 GHz.
elements.sent-2411-003: As expected, photon counters are found to be advantageous under low background, at high frequencies ($\nu>$ 5 GHz), {\em if they can be implemented with robust wide-frequency tuning or a very low dark count rate.}
elements.sent-2411-004: Additional noteworthy observations emerging from this study include: (1) an expanded applicability of off-resonance photon background reduction, including the single-quadrature state squeezing, for scan rate enhancements; (2) a much broader appeal for operating the haloscope resonators in the over-coupling regime, up to $\beta\sim 10$; (3) the need for a detailed investigation into the cryogenic and electromagnetic conditions inside haloscope cavities to lower the photon temperature for future experiments; (4) the necessity to develop a distributed network of coupling ports in high-volume axion haloscopes to utilize these potential gains in the scan rate.
elements.sent-2411-005: QCD axions are excellent candidates for the cosmological dark matter \cite{PecciQuinn1977,Weinberg1978,Wilczek1978,abbott83,marsh .}
elements.sent-2411-006: Their presence in a terrestrial laboratory can be detected using a well-established method, the cavity-based haloscope \cite{sikivie1,admx18,haystac18,capp .}
elements.sent-2411-007: In this scheme, a cavity resonator is immersed in an external dc magnetic field, resonantly converting halo axions into a microwave signal.
elements.sent-2411-008: The resonant frequency of the cavity can be tuned to search for a narrow line which would indicate the presence of axion dark matter.
elements.sent-2411-009: The cavity is coupled to a low-noise amplifier for readout and data processing.
elements.sent-2411-010: In recent years, advances in quantum sensing techniques enable new capabilities in axion dark matter searches\cite{lamoreaux,sushkov .}
elements.sent-2411-011: It is now possible to apply vacuum squeezing techniques to evade the standard quantum limit (SQL) by using a pair of parametric amplifiers operating in a single-quadrature squeezed state setup \cite{malnou,backes,jewell .}
elements.sent-2411-012: It has also been demonstrated that a microwave photon counter can be used in cm-wave axion dark matter searches \cite{lescanne,albertinale,balembois,braggio , which enhances the scan rate by avoiding SQL altogether.}
elements.sent-2411-013: In this paper, we provide an in-depth comparison of the linear amplifiers and the photon-counting microwave detectors in axion dark matter searches.
elements.sent-2411-014: Revisiting this subject is timely given these advances in quantum information technologies.
elements.sent-2411-015: Obviously, such promises can only be realized when auxiliary systems in the haloscope experiments can support operation of these new sensors under appropriate conditions.
elements.sent-2411-016: The purpose of this review is to clarify what these requirements are and to develop a road map for future axion dark matter searches in the frequency range of 1 -- 30 GHz, corresponding to the post-inflationary scenario of axion production \cite{marsh,Borsanyi2016,Klaer_2017,Graham_2018,Takahashi_2018,Buschmann2022 .}
elements.sent-2411-017: The paper is organized as follows.
elements.sent-2411-018: In \ref{sec:scanrate we derive the scan rate of an axion haloscope read out by either a linear amplifier (with the option of adding a squeezing de-amplifier) or a single microwave photon detector.}
elements.sent-2411-019: We obtain closed-form expressions of which we take various limits -- photon noise background- versus dark count rate (DCR)- limits, as functions of the temperature and search frequencies.
elements.sent-2411-020: In \ref{sec:compare , we proceed to use the results to comprehensively compare these two readout systems, and their ability to reach theoretical benchmarks such as the KSVZ \cite{kim,shifman and DFSZ \cite{Zhitnitsky:1980tq,DINE1981199 .}}}
elements.sent-2411-021: We conclude in \ref{sec:conclude and discuss the implications of these results on future searches of the post-inflationary dark matter axions.}

elements.sent-2411-026: The measurement port $m$ is connected to a circulator whose output goes to a low noise amplifier to the right and the input from an impedance matching termination to the left, with the efficiency $\lambda\leq 1$ in either direction.

elements.sent-2411-030: This situation arises in axion experiments because the large surface area of the haloscope cavity and the poorly thermal anchored tuning elements often make it difficult to operate the haloscope at the base temperature $T_b$ of the mixing chamber \cite{backes,jewell .}

elements.sent-2411-032: Starting with the Heisenberg-Langevin equation for the cavity ladder operator $\hat A$ and following the steps based on known solutions to the input-output theory as presented in Appendix A of \cite{malnou , we obtain the single-quadrature spectral density at the amplifier: \begin{equation}\label{sigma} \Sigma_{out,X,m}=(n_T+\frac{1}{2})\ G_a\ (1-\lambda)+\frac{G_a\ \lambda}{B(\omega)}\times \Biggl[(n_A+\frac{1}{2}) \kappa_a \kappa_m+(n_T+\frac{1}{2}) \kappa_l \kappa_m +(n_b+\frac{1}{2})(1-\lambda+\lambda/G_s )A(\omega)\Biggr],\end{equation} where $A(\omega)\equiv\omega^2+(\kappa_m-\kappa_l)^2/4$ ; $B(\omega)\equiv\omega^2+(\kappa_m+\kappa_l)^2/4 \ \ $; and the photon occupation number $n$ for thermal sources is given by $(e^{\hbar \omega/kT -1)^{-1}$.}}
elements.sent-2411-033: The coefficients $A(\omega)$ is simply the resonator reflectivity $|\Gamma|^2$ in classical microwave theory and $B(\omega)$ a normalization factor.
elements.sent-2411-034: The coupling constants $\kappa_m,\kappa_l$ can be connected with their microwave equivalents by the following identifications: $\kappa_l/\omega_c=\kappa_l/(2\pi \nu_c)=1/Q_0$ and $\kappa_m=\beta\kappa_l$, where $Q_0$ is the intrinsic (unloaded) quality factor of the resonator and $\beta$ is the coupling parameter of the measurement port.

elements.sent-2411-039: We reproduce their Eqns B(10) \& B(11) here \begin{equation}\label{na} n_A=\frac{\lvert\gagg\rvert\rho_a B_0V}{4\omega_a\Delta_a}\sqrt{\frac{C_{mnl}c^3}{\hbar\mu_0}}\end{equation} \begin{equation}\label{ka} \kappa_a= \lvert\gagg\rvert B_0\sqrt{\frac{C_{mnl}\hbar c^3}{\mu_0}}\end{equation} The parameters $\gagg,\rho_a, B_0, C_{mnl}$ are the axion-photon coupling strength, local axion density, external magnetic field, and a geometrical ``form factor" for the eigenmode, respectively.

elements.figure-2411-005: \includegraphics[width=1 \columnwidth]{smpd_sketch.png} \caption{The SMPD \cite{braggio,lescanne,balembois,albertinale} consists of an intake resonator (``buffer") that defines the band of the incoming photons, a resonator that defines the outgoing mode (``waste"), and a transmon qubit coupled to both resonators.
